name: Divine Will
shortDescription: The Divine Will in Babylonian mythology refers to the preordained plans and intentions of the gods that govern the cosmos and human affairs. It was believed that understanding and aligning with this will was crucial for success and prosperity.
description: The concept of Divine Will in Babylonian mythology was central to their understanding of the universe and their place within it. It represented the preordained plans and intentions of the gods, particularly the chief deities such as Marduk, Ea, and Shamash, which dictated the course of events in both the cosmic and human realms. This divine will was not necessarily seen as immutable fate but rather as a complex web of possibilities that could be influenced, though not entirely controlled, through proper ritual actions, prayers, and divination. Understanding the divine will was paramount, as aligning oneself with it was believed to bring success, prosperity, and protection, while acting against it courted misfortune and divine displeasure. Divination practices, such as extispicy (examining the entrails of sacrificed animals) and astrology, were developed as means to decipher the intentions of the gods and gain insights into the divine plan. Priests and diviners played a crucial role in interpreting these signs and advising individuals and the state on how to act in accordance with the divine will. The concept wasn't always monolithic; different gods might have different intentions or priorities, and the divine will could be interpreted in multiple ways, leading to complex theological debates and interpretations. The Enuma Elish, the Babylonian creation epic, illustrates how Marduk, after defeating Tiamat, established order in the cosmos and decreed the fates of the gods and humanity, solidifying the concept of a divine plan guiding the universe. While not explicitly defined as a single entity, the Divine Will was the underlying force shaping Babylonian society and individual lives.

sources.title: The Ancient Near East: An Anthology of Texts and Pictures
sources.author: James B. Pritchard

sources.title: Enuma Elish: The Epic of Creation
sources.author: Stephanie Dalley
